message:
harden(fcp-crypto): remove Clone from FrostSigningNonces to enforce single-use at compile time (RFC 9591 §6.3)

FROST (RFC 9591 §6.3) is unambiguous about nonce reuse: the
per-participant signing nonces from round 1 MUST be used in exactly
one round-2 `sign` call. Reusing a nonce commitment across two
different messages lets any observer of the two signature shares
recover the participant's signing share outright — a complete loss
of threshold security for that participant, not just a confidentiality
degradation.

`FrostSigningNonces` previously derived `Clone`. The `sign` function
already consumes the nonces by move, giving us a compile-time
guarantee that a single Rust ownership path cannot reuse them. But
a `.clone()` call on a `&FrostSigningNonces` silently bypassed that
guarantee: now two callers hold owned copies, each can call `sign`
independently against a different message, and nobody has triggered
a single warning. That is precisely the attack RFC 9591 tells us to
protect against.

This commit:

1. Removes `Clone` from the derive list. Any caller that was
   cloning nonces for convenience will now get a compile error
   naming the site, which is the right failure mode: every such
   site is a potential nonce-reuse bug waiting for bad luck to
   turn it into a real one. We want reviewers to look at each.

2. Keeps `PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize, Zeroize,
   ZeroizeOnDrop` unchanged. Serialize/Deserialize matter: a
   participant legitimately needs to persist nonces to disk between
   round 1 and round 2 on a single machine, or send them across a
   process boundary (e.g., an enclave → userspace handoff). That
   is one-way transport, not duplication — the caller is
   responsible for ensuring the nonces flow through at most one
   `sign` call total across those hops, which is the same
   invariant the type system now enforces in-memory.

3. Adds a detailed SECURITY doc comment on the struct explaining
   the full reasoning: the single-use rule from RFC 9591 §6.3, why
   move-consume in `sign` is the compile-time enforcement
   mechanism, why Clone is deliberately absent, and why Serialize/
   Deserialize remain. This doc comment is load-bearing: anyone
   tempted to re-add Clone for ergonomic reasons now has to walk
   past the explanation of what they are trading away.

Zero runtime cost. Any existing clone-based use case (callers who
genuinely need two owned copies for legitimate reasons, like a
retry layer holding a backup) has to be refactored to explicitly
generate *fresh* nonces via the round-1 protocol, which is the
correct behavior under RFC 9591 anyway — two sign calls must use
two distinct nonce commitments, full stop.

files.patch:
@@ -315,7 +315,20 @@ impl std::fmt::Debug for FrostDkgRound2Package {
 
 /// Secret FROST signing nonces retained by a participant between round 1 and
 /// round 2 of threshold signing.
-#[derive(Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize, Zeroize, ZeroizeOnDrop)]
+///
+/// **SECURITY (FROST nonce-reuse rule, RFC 9591 §6.3):** these nonces MUST
+/// be used for exactly one `sign` call. Reuse across two different messages
+/// lets any observer of both signature shares recover the participant's
+/// signing share — catastrophic for threshold security.
+///
+/// `sign` consumes this value by move to enforce single-use at compile
+/// time. `Clone` is intentionally NOT derived: handing two callers a
+/// cloned copy would defeat the move-consume guarantee and re-open the
+/// nonce-reuse attack. `Serialize`/`Deserialize` remain so a participant
+/// can persist nonces between rounds on a single machine (or pass them
+/// across process boundaries once), but the caller is responsible for
+/// the "once" invariant across those hops.
+#[derive(PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize, Zeroize, ZeroizeOnDrop)]
 pub struct FrostSigningNonces {
     #[zeroize(skip)]
     participant: u16,
